title: When The Test Cell Lies
gist: As AI/HPC dies move into expensive advanced packages, separating real device failures from test-cell artifacts (socket, load board, thermal loop, calibration drift) has become a first-order economic problem, because a false pass destroys downstream value, a false failure scraps good silicon, and a false downgrade quietly hides lost value inside normal binning.

insights.claim: Separating device failures from test-cell artifacts has become a first-order economic problem as margins shrink and dies move into expensive advanced packages, not merely an engineering nuisance.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Test accuracy is now a direct driver of margin for advanced-packaging and chiplet supply chains; watch test-and-inspection vendors (Teradyne, Advantest, Keysight) as beneficiaries of this spend shift.
insights.quote: As margins shrink and dies move into expensive packages, separating device failures from test-cell artifacts has become a first-order economic problem.
insights.locator: As margins shrink and dies move

insights.claim: In advanced packages a false pass destroys value downstream, a false failure discards value early, and a false downgrade hides the loss inside normal binning without ever looking like a yield event.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The costliest test errors are invisible in yield reports; investors should discount headline yield numbers for advanced packages unless margin/downgrade tracking is in place.
insights.quote: a false pass destroys value later, a false failure discards value earlier, and a false downgrade removes value without ever looking like a yield event.
insights.locator: That matters most for known-good die

insights.claim: A marginal test result can be electrically valid yet diagnostically misleading because the socket, load board, and thermal loop are now part of the measurement, not neutral infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Any test result must be read together with the health of the measurement chain; frames demand for interconnect, socket, and metrology suppliers.
insights.quote: A bad contact, a drifting channel, a hot probe card, or an unrepresentative workload can produce a result that is electrically valid but diagnostically misleading.
insights.locator: It's not just an instrumentation question

insights.claim: The first useful diagnostic question is not what failed but what path the failure follows: if a device fails across sites, boards, and testers the device is the suspect, but if the failure stays with a site, channel, socket, or handler position the test cell is implicated.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Diagnostic value comes from cross-referencing results against equipment and location, which favors analytics platforms that preserve those relationships (PDF Solutions, tester OEMs).
insights.quote: The clearest indicator that a failure is tester-side, rather than device-side, is the failure stays attached to the same instrument channel, slot, or test site even as different devices rotate through it
insights.locator: Two failure paths diverged in the woods

insights.claim: In a properly operating high-site-count system yield should be similar across all sites, so significant site-to-site yield differences flag a test-cell issue rather than a device problem.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Site-level yield divergence is an early, cheap tell of test-cell degradation worth monitoring before scrapping silicon.
insights.quote: the expectation is that yield will be similar across all sites, so any significant differences will indicate a potential issue with the test cell.
insights.locator: That sounds straightforward until it happens

insights.claim: Large AI and HPC dies shrink the number of die per wafer, making it harder to build a clean statistical picture before yield decisions come due.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Big-die AI/HPC economics structurally weaken traditional test statistics, raising the value of context-aware and cross-lot analytics.
insights.quote: large AI and HPC die shrink the number of die per wafer, making it harder to build a clean statistical picture before yield decisions come due.
insights.locator: That sounds straightforward until it happens

insights.claim: Physical substitution — swapping sockets or instruments across sites and watching whether the error follows the hardware or stays with the location — remains a durable diagnostic even in data-rich flows.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Old-school swap tests still gate expensive silicon decisions; not everything is solved by software analytics.
insights.quote: Swap sockets or instruments across sites, and watch whether the error follows the hardware or stays with the location.
insights.locator: The device is only one piece

insights.claim: The socket, probe card, or contactor is electrically part of the measurement — a mechanical system used as an electrical interconnect that wears, heats, and contaminates, turning marginal contacts into product-quality issues rather than maintenance issues.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Interconnect quality is a product-quality lever; supports demand for socket/contactor and contact-resistance monitoring vendors as margins tighten.
insights.quote: It's a mechanical system that's being used as an electrical interconnect, and it's got any number of mechanical defects that can occur from the actual physical wear, plating wearing off, springs getting weak
insights.locator: The interface is part of the measurement

insights.claim: A bad interface does not have to reject a part to do damage; it can bin good silicon into a lower frequency or voltage grade, so the failure never appears as scrap and instead shows up as quietly lost value.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Undetected downgrade erodes ASP on premium parts invisibly; contact-resistance signature tracking directly protects revenue mix.
insights.quote: It gets binned into a lower grade in frequency or voltage, so it will still pass the test, but it will be sorted into a lower bin.
insights.locator: Everything boils down to contact resistance signature

insights.claim: Compromised power delivery through the interface can make a device appear to have fewer usable cores or less frequency headroom than the silicon would deliver under a better connection, an error that is dangerous because it looks plausible.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Core/frequency downgrades from bad connections mimic real silicon limits; matters for high-value multi-core AI/HPC parts where bin value is steep.
insights.quote: A device may appear to have fewer usable cores or less frequency headroom than the silicon would deliver under a better connection.
insights.locator: The same ambiguity appears when power delivery

insights.claim: Pass/fail information is becoming insufficient as a test result; knowing by how much a device passed and whether that margin is stable is more useful than the binary verdict.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Shift toward margin/variance logging expands the market for richer test-data analytics beyond simple bin reporting.
insights.quote: Knowing that a device passed is less useful than knowing by how much it passed and whether that margin is stable
insights.locator: Margins, calibration, and variance

insights.claim: Variance rather than mean is the earliest tell of test-cell drift — creeping retest rates, expanding marginal bins, and two sites that used to agree beginning to separate signal the measurement path has become part of the problem.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Monitoring variance trends, not just averages, is the leading indicator of measurement degradation; a screen worth building into fab analytics.
insights.quote: 'Within specification' is a pass/fail verdict, and drift is a trend. The earliest tell is usually variance, not mean.
insights.locator: This is the trap

insights.claim: A test cell can remain within formal calibration limits yet become less trustworthy precisely at the boundary where the business decision is made, so the calibration record should be treated as continuous diagnostic evidence rather than a compliance artifact.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: In-spec calibration is not a guarantee of decision-quality measurement; argues for continuous calibration-trend tracking tooling.
insights.quote: a test cell can still be within formal limits while becoming less trustworthy precisely at the boundary where the business decision is made.
insights.locator: As a diagnostic, the calibration record

insights.claim: A parametric offset that appears after extended run time and disappears after a calibration routine implicates the instrument or out-of-spec operation, making calibration scheduling itself a diagnostic signal.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Correlating drift against cal cycles is a low-cost way to localize test-cell faults; feeds analytics-platform value.
insights.quote: If a parametric offset tracks the tester's calibration cycle, appearing after extended run time and disappearing after a cal routine, the instrument is implicated but not necessarily faulty.
insights.locator: Calibration scheduling is also diagnostic

themes.theme: Power and heat as test conditions

insights.claim: In high-power AI/HPC devices the junction temperature during test can deviate significantly from the chuck or socket ambient, so a device can be tested under a condition that looks controlled from outside the package but is not controlled where the failure mechanism actually lives.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Thermal observability at the junction is becoming a differentiator for testers; on-die sensor integration is a growth vector for ATE and IP vendors.
insights.quote: The junction temperature during test can deviate significantly from the chuck or socket ambient.
insights.locator: Thermal observability inside the test cell

insights.claim: High current through a marginal contact self-heats: enough IR drop causes thermal runaway that can physically melt things, especially without strong protection mechanisms.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Socket condition is a physical risk to expensive devices under test; reliability of contactors is an underappreciated cost driver for high-power parts.
insights.quote: If you get enough IR drop there, it will start self-heating, and it will thermally run away and actually melt things
insights.locator: The interface has its own version

insights.claim: Thermal and power interactions rarely leave stable signatures — a device passes cold and fails after sustained activity, a load board shifts as it heats, a supply transient intermittently resets the device — making swept debug conditions as important as the failing test itself.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Intermittent thermal/power artifacts drive demand for condition-sweeping and waveform-level debug capability in test flows.
insights.quote: A device passes cold and fails after sustained activity. A load board's behavior shifts as components heat. A supply transient intermittently resets a device.
insights.locator: These interactions rarely leave stable signatures

insights.claim: Inspection and metrology (X-ray, acoustic, optical/3D AOI) act as independent non-destructive witnesses that confirm whether an electrical symptom has a physical counterpart — a co-located structural anomaly makes the product the suspect, while contact-height, contamination, warpage, or coplanarity findings point back to the test interface.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Inspection is becoming a required corroborating layer for advanced-package test; expands the served market for X-ray/acoustic/AOI vendors like Nordson.
insights.quote: If X-ray, acoustic, or optical inspection reveals a structural anomaly in the same location as the electrical failure, the product itself becomes the stronger suspect.
insights.locator: Independent witnesses

insights.claim: On-die sensors testify from inside the package, giving engineers a voltage and temperature reference that does not depend on the health of anything between the instrument and the die, letting them tell a real device droop from a power-delivery or socket-resistance artifact.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: In-package telemetry (proteanTecs-style) directly disambiguates test-cell vs device faults, a structural tailwind for embedded monitoring IP.
insights.quote: If the on-chip voltage sensors show a stable supply rail while the tester reports a droop, the droop is in the power delivery chain outside the device.
insights.locator: The package boundary has always been the limit of observability

insights.claim: In silicon/silicon-germanium nanosheet stacks the germanium concentration defines the tensile strain in the silicon channels, and interfacial roughness impacts electron mobility, so out-of-spec Ge content or roughness produces marginal electrical behavior long after the deposition could have been corrected.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Front-end film metrology (Bruker X-ray) is upstream leverage on later electrical marginality; ties advanced-node process control to test outcomes.
insights.quote: The germanium content in the SiGe film defines the tensile strain in the silicon channels
insights.locator: Meanwhile, tiny differences in chemical concentration

insights.claim: Front-end metrology results should be handed off to test engineers because a wafer that was within control limits but closer to a boundary than expected is valuable context when the same material later produces marginal electrical behavior.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Cross-domain data handoff (front-end metrology to back-end test) is an emerging integration gap and opportunity for data-chain platforms.
insights.quote: If I were a test engineer, it would be helpful to have access to front-end results from that batch.
insights.locator: Front-end metrology is a key control measure

insights.claim: Front-end metrology, wafer sort, package inspection, final test, retest, and field data live in different systems with different owners, and that fragmentation is no longer tolerable when distinguishing a good die from a marginal die from a test-cell artifact depends on correlating data across multiple insertions and physical domains.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The unmet need is unified cross-insertion data correlation, the core value proposition of platforms like PDF Solutions Exensio.
insights.quote: Front-end metrology, wafer sort, package inspection, final test, retest, and field data often live in different systems with different owners.
insights.locator: Data that knows what it followed

insights.claim: A retest that preserves device identity, original site, failing test, and recovered bin becomes diagnostic evidence — moving 100 failed devices to a different tester and seeing bin 25 become bin 1 for 85% of them is near-certain proof of a setup issue, meaning good silicon was being left on the table.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Concrete recovery example quantifies the value of retest-tracking analytics; a sales argument for data-chain infrastructure.
insights.quote: took them over to a different tester, ran them again, and bin 25 became bin 1 for 85% of the time, now I know I had a setup issue. It's almost certain
insights.locator: If I had 100 devices that didn't pass

insights.claim: Traditional statistics assume enough observations to establish normal, but die counts per wafer are shrinking while site counts climb, so a single wafer or lot may not contain enough information on its own to separate product variation from test-cell variation — potentially needing ~50 data points per site across 16 sites before judging devices.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The statistical baseline problem forces reliance on cross-lot/historical context, favoring big-data test analytics over per-lot heuristics.
insights.quote: If we run 16 sites, I might need 50 data points per site before I start judging the devices under test.
insights.locator: For the most part, people don't consider context

insights.claim: Adaptive test and AI analytics are both promising and risky because they learn from whatever they are fed: if a socket, channel, or thermal loop injects noise into the raw data, an adaptive system can mistake test-cell behavior for device behavior and bake that misunderstanding into future decisions.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: AI test analytics is gated on clean measurement foundations; noisy sockets undermine model value, so data-quality tooling is a prerequisite investment.
insights.quote: if a socket, channel, or thermal loop is injecting noise into the raw data, an adaptive system can mistake test-cell behavior for device behavior and build that misunderstanding into future decisions.
insights.locator: That's one reason adaptive test

themes.theme: Guard-bands and test as process intelligence

insights.claim: Test is shifting from a screen to a source of process intelligence, now expected not just to protect throughput, cost, and quality but to explain why a device behaved the way it did — mattering most for known-good die and advanced packages.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Test's expanding role as an intelligence source enlarges the addressable value of analytics-rich test over commodity pass/fail screening.
insights.quote: The broader shift is that test is moving from a screen to a source of process intelligence.
insights.locator: Conclusion

insights.claim: A guard-band is a business risk decision about uncertainty, escape risk, overkill, and downstream cost rather than a formula — and if the dominant uncertainty comes from an untracked contactor, fixture, or thermal condition, the guard-band may be hiding a problem that should be fixed directly.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Guard-banding decisions in the chiplet era are economic, not technical; over-conservative bands mask correctable test-cell faults and destroy value.
insights.quote: Once you have a defensible expanded uncertainty, the guard-band is a risk decision, not a formula. Set the limit to the consequence, not to the convenience.
insights.locator: The same pressure is changing how guard

insights.claim: In the chiplet era a bad die does not fail alone — it takes an expensive assembly with it — which multiplies the cost of any test escape by everything attached to it.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Escape cost scales with assembly value in chiplet designs, justifying higher test/inspection spend per die for advanced packages.
insights.quote: In a chiplet era, a bad die doesn't fail alone, it takes an expensive assembly with it.
insights.locator: The same pressure is changing how guard

insights.claim: Customers can no longer afford to throw away a device larger than 10x10 centimeters just because one single die is not performing correctly, and now want more test coverage distributed across the chain from wafer to package.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Demand is moving toward distributed wafer-to-package test coverage, expanding tester and system-level-test spend per large device.
insights.quote: Customers can no longer afford the luxury of throwing away a device larger than 10 x 10 centimeters just because one single die is not performing correctly
insights.locator: Conclusion
